sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 56, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 55 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the left green square moved from (28, 19) to about (28, 22). The right green square stayed at (31, 40). So when the left green square is at (28, 19), RIGHT controls the left green square, not the right green square.
This is different from when the left green square was at (31, 19), where RIGHT controlled the right green square. So the control scheme depends on the left green square's row position:
- When left green is at row 31, LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square.
- When left green is at row 28, LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square.
And UP/DOWN always controls the left green square (based on earlier observations).
So the control scheme is:
- UP/DOWN: always controls the left green square
- LEFT/RIGHT: controls the left green square when it's at row 28, controls the right green square when it's at row 31
Wait, but earlier when the left green square was at (34, 16), LEFT/RIGHT controlled the left green square. And when the left green square was at (31, 10), LEFT/RIGHT controlled the left green square. So the rule is not simply based on the row.
Let me reconsider. When the left green square is at (31, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square. When the left green square is at (28, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square. When the left green square is at (34, 16), LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square. When the left green square is at (31, 10), LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square.
Hmm, so the only time LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square is when the left green square is at (31, 19). What's special about (31, 19)?
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine. When the left green square is at (31, 19), it's in the middle of the left frame horizontally (cols 11-22, center 16.5, so 19 is to the right of center). And when the left green square is at (31, 10), it's outside the left frame (col 10 < 11).
But when the left green square is at (34, 16), it's inside the left frame (cols 11-22, row 34 is below row 31). And LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square.
So the rule might be:
- When the left green square is at row 31 and col >= 19 (or some threshold), LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square.
- Otherwise, LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square.
But this is getting complicated. Let me just focus on the goal: getting both green squares into their respective frames.
Currently:
- Left green square: (28, 19) - need to check if it's inside the left frame
- Right green square: (31, 40) - need to move it to the right frame at (28, 46)
The left frame is at (34, 13), which means the left frame border is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15. The left green square at (28, 19) is above and to the right of the left frame. So the left green square is NOT inside the left frame.
Wait, but earlier I saw the left green square at (34, 16) inside the left frame. Let me check the left frame's position more carefully.
From the segmentation:
- c border: px=16, center=(34, 13), boundary=[[32, 11], [32, 15], [36, 15], [36, 11]]
- w inside: px=9, center=(34, 13), boundary=[[33, 12], [33, 14], [35, 14], [35, 12]]
So the left frame border is at rows 32-36, cols 11-15. The white square inside is at rows 33-35, cols 12-14.
The left green square at (28, 19) is at rows 27-29, cols 18-20. This is above and to the right of the left frame. So the left green square is NOT inside the left frame.
To get the left green square into the left frame, I need to move it DOWN to row 34 and LEFT to col 13.
Currently, the left green square is at (28, 19). To move it to (34, 13):
- DOWN 2 times (from row 28 to row 34)
- LEFT 2 times (from col 19 to col 13)
But wait, when the left green square is at (28, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square. So I can move it LEFT and DOWN.
Let me try moving the left green square DOWN 2 times and LEFT 2 times to get it into the left frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'w', 'c', 'p'):
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        if n['pixels'] <= 20 or n['color'] == 'p':
            print(f"  color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, center={center}")

# Left green at (28, 22), need to move to (34, 13) to be inside left frame
# Move DOWN 2 times (row 28 -> 34) and LEFT 2 times (col 22 -> 13)
# But first, let me check if LEFT/RIGHT controls left green when it's at (28, 22)
# From earlier, when left green is at (28, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls left green
# So let's try moving DOWN and LEFT

action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
color=p, px=126, center=(31, 35)
  color=c, px=16, center=(28, 46)
  color=N, px=8, center=(28, 22)
  color=w, px=9, center=(28, 46)
  color=N, px=8, center=(31, 40)
  color=c, px=1, center=(31, 40)
  color=c, px=16, center=(34, 13)
  color=w, px=9, center=(34, 13)
